  <thead><tr><th style='text-align: center;'>step ( *E+02 )</th><th style='text-align: center;'>ESTIMATED</th><th style='text-align: center;'>TRUE</th></tr></thead>

    <tr><td style='text-align: center;'>0.01</td><td style='text-align: center;'>0.000001</td><td style='text-align: center;'>0.000001</td></tr>

<div style="text-align: center;">FIGURE 5.4: Behavior of the error-estimator (Example 5.1).</div>


Example 5.2: Free Radical Polymerization. We return to Example 1.4 now, where we discussed the behavior of the radical polymers during the initial phase of a free radical polymerization process. A (slightly simplified) modeling of the kinetics of the radical polymers leads to

 $$ \begin{array}{rcl}u_{1}^{\prime}(t)&=&-k_{p}(t)\;u_{1}(t)+f(t)\;,\\u_{s}^{\prime}(t)&=&-k_{p}(t)\;(u_{s}(t)-u_{s-1}(t))-k_{t}\;u_{s}(t)\end{array} $$ 

with  $ u_{s}(t) $ denoting the concentration of the radical polymer s. The reaction coefficients are

 $$ k_{p}(t)=e^{-0.01t}\quad,\quad k_{t}=5\cdot10^{-2}\quad. $$ 

An initiation process is modeled by

 $$ f(t)=I_{0}\;k_{d}\;e^{-k_{d}t}\;,\;I_{0}=1.0\cdot10^{-2}\;,\;k_{d}=10^{-1}\;. $$ 

The reaction constants are chosen such that (5.1) reflects the most important structures of the complete polymerization system at the beginning of the reaction. As the solution tends to a nearly stationary state, the implicit Euler discretization has been used here. The analytical preprocessing is not changed by the time dependent reaction coefficients.

Figure 5.5 presents a simulation up to t = 100 sec. This time is comparable to t = 5 sec in the discussion of the original process (Example 1.4). The solution starts as a geometric distribution ( $ \rho = 0.1 $), approaches a Poisson distribution with parameter  $ \lambda \sim t $, and finally returns to a geometric distribution with  $ \rho \to 1 $. This is one possible (but not the only) behavior of a free radical
